elements.section-2411-004: Lower Bounds for Regret Minimization in Stochastic Rising Bandits
elements.sent-2411-093: In this section, we provide a set of regret lower bounds highlighting the inherent and statistical challenges of the regret minimization problem in stochastic rising bandits.

elements.sent-2411-393: We showed that an assumption on the expected reward (\eg concavity) is essential to make the problem learnable.
elements.sent-2411-394: However, we also showed that, in the most general case, the rising rested concave environment is still unlearnable and we inferred under which conditions it is possible to obtain a sublinear lower bound on the regret.
elements.sent-2411-395: We presented a novel algorithm that suitably employs the concavity assumption to build proper estimators for the setting.
elements.sent-2411-396: The experimental evaluation confirmed our theoretical findings showing advantages over state-of-the-art algorithms designed for non-stationary bandits.
elements.sent-2411-397: Among the many interesting future lines of research starting from this work, we mention the possibility of designing effective algorithms if the increasing nature of the problem is somehow constrained and the study of cases in which the arms reward presents a structure among them.
elements.sent-2411-398: The former case includes the cases in which the increment is known to the learner, or we have stricter conditions than the concavity, \eg strong concavity.
elements.sent-2411-399: In such a setting, it is an open problem to show the lower bounds dependence and the design of algorithms explicitly exploiting such an additional structure.
elements.sent-2411-400: In the latter case, the research may consider standard structure among arms, \eg linear bandit or Gaussian process bandit structure, to exploit in a more efficient way the information coming from the entire set of arms, while still considering the rising nature of the problem.
